title: 5 Steps Startups Should Follow for Successful Product Development
author: Caylin White

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: You have a brilliant idea. A solution that will change how people work, play, or live. But an idea, no matter how great, is just the beginning. The journey from concept to a successful, market-ready product is paved with critical decisions and a lot of hard work. Forstartups, where every resource counts, a misstep in product development can be fatal. The key to success isn’t just about building a product; it’s about building therightproduct. A product that solves a real problem for real people, in a way that’s better than the competition. At Salesforce, we’ve seen countless startups grow from a handful of people with an idea into thriving, customer-centric businesses. We’ve learned that the most successful ones follow a disciplined, customer-focused approach. So here are five steps every startup should follow for successful product development. What is product development and why does your startup need it?Five product development steps to follow for successCommon startup product development strategiesAnd, 3 tips for successful product developmentRecommended tools for product developmentReady to build, scale, and grow?

sections.heading: What is product development and why does your startup need it?
sections.text: Product development is the entire process of bringing a new product or service to market. For startups, it’s crucial because it allows them to: Solve a problem:Startups are often born out of identifying an unmet need or a problem that existing solutions don’t adequately address. Product development is how they build something to solve that problem.Gain a competitive edge:A well-developed, innovative product can differentiate a startup from larger, more established companies.Attract investment:Investors look for startups with a clear vision and a viable product. A solid product development plan demonstrates that a startup knows how to execute its ideas.Achieve market fit:Through product development, startups can iterate and refine their offerings based on user feedback, ensuring the product truly resonates with its target audience.Generate revenue:Ultimately, successful product development leads to a product that customers want to buy, which is essential for a startup’s survival and growth.

sections.heading: Five product development steps to follow for success
sections.text: Instead of relying on guesswork or limited data, startups can useartificial intelligence (AI)to conduct robustmarket research. AI tools can analyze vast datasets from social media, customer reviews, forums, and survey results to identify trends, pinpoint customer pain points, and segment target audiences with high accuracy. This allows you to validate your product idea and ensure you’re solving a real-world problem for a clearly defined group of people, saving you from building a product no one wants. Once you’ve validated your idea, AI can act as a co-pilot for the creative process. Generative AI tools can create multiple design variations and prototypes in minutes based on simple text prompts. You can quickly explore different user interfaces (UI), user experiences (UX), and even product concepts without a team of designers or costly software. This rapid prototyping reduces the time and cost of the early development phase, allowing you to iterate quickly and get to a functionalminimum viable product (MVP)faster. AI isn’t just for planning and design; it can also accelerate the build phase.Vibe codingtools can generate code snippets, suggest optimizations, and even create entire applications based on your design specifications. This can significantly reduce development time and effort, especially for small teams with limited coding expertise. While a human is still needed to oversee and refine the code, AI handles the repetitive, time-consuming parts, allowing your developers to focus on complex, high-value tasks. Manual testing is a bottleneck for many small businesses. AI-poweredquality assurance (QA)tools can automate repetitive testing tasks, such as regression testing and visual UI comparisons. AI can also predict where bugs are most likely to occur by analyzing code changes and historical data, allowing your team to prioritize testing in high-risk areas. This leads to a more reliable product, fewer post-launch issues, and a faster time to market. The product development journey doesn’t end at launch. After your product is in the hands of customers, AI can provide a continuous feedback loop. AI tools can analyzecustomer reviews,support tickets, and social media mentions in real-time. Usingnatural language processing (NLP), AI can identify key themes, track sentiment, and categorize feedback automatically. This gives you a clear, data-driven understanding of what customers love and what needs improvement, allowing you to make informed decisions for future product updates and build a product that your customers will continue to love.

sections.heading: Common startup product development strategies
sections.text: Successful startups don’t just build a product and hope for the best. They follow a strategic approach to product development that maximizes their limited resources and focuses on what truly matters: the customer. Here are three of the most common and effective product development strategies for startups. The Lean Startup, popularized by entrepreneurEric Ries, is a methodology that prioritizes speed and learning over extensive planning. It is built on a “Build-Measure-Learn” feedback loop that helps startups quickly validate their ideas and pivot when necessary. Build:Instead of building a final, feature-complete product, the startup creates an MVP. This is the simplest version of the product with just enough features to be usable by early customers.Measure:The startup then releases the MVP to a small group of users and collects data and feedback. This is a crucial step to “validate learning”—determining if the product is solving a real problem for the target audience.Learn:Based on the data and feedback, the startup learns what’s working and what isn’t. This learning informs the next steps, which could be to “persevere” (continue building on the current path) or “pivot” (change direction based on the new insights). The goal of the Lean Startup is to “fail fast,” meaning that if an idea is not viable, you discover it quickly and cheaply, rather than after months or years of wasted effort. Instead of a rigid, linear process (like the “waterfall” method), Agile breaks down the development work into small, manageable chunks called “sprints” or “iterations.” Iterate:The team works in short cycles (typically one to four weeks) to deliver a working, usable increment of the product. This continuous delivery of small features allows for rapid feedback and adaptation.Collaborate:A key principle of Agile is constant communication and collaboration with customers. This ensures that the product is always aligned with their evolving needs.Adapt:The Agile Manifestostates, “Responding to change over following a plan.” This mindset allows startups to quickly incorporate new information, market shifts, and user feedback into their product roadmap without derailing the entire project. Agile allows startups to release a product to market faster and reduce the risk of building something that doesn’t meet user requirements. Design Thinkingis a human-centered problem-solving framework that puts the end-user at the heart of the product development process. It’s less about a rigid process and more about a mindset that encourages creative and innovative solutions. The process typically involves five stages: Empathize:The startup immerses itself in the user’s world to understand their needs, challenges, and motivations. This can involve interviews, observations, and creating user personas.Define:Based on the empathy phase, the startup synthesizes the information to create a clear, actionable problem statement. This ensures the team is focused on solving the right problem.Ideate:This is a brainstorming phase where the team generates a wide range of creative ideas to solve the defined problem. No idea is too crazy, and the goal is to produce a high volume of potential solutions.Prototype:The most promising ideas are turned into low-fidelity prototypes (like a paper sketch, a simple wireframe, or a basic model) that can be quickly tested.Test:The prototypes are tested with real users to gather feedback and refine the solution. This is an iterative cycle, and it’s common to go back to an earlier stage (ideation or prototyping) based on the test results.

sections.heading: And, 3 tips for successful product development

Instead of trying to solve a dozen problems with an average solution, identify the single, most painful problem your target audience faces and create a product that provides the best possible solution to that specific issue. This singular focus allows you to channel your limited resources — time, money, and talent — into creating a product that is truly indispensable. Once you’ve dominated that niche and established a loyal user base, you can strategically add more features to expand your product’s capabilities. Your ideas are great, but the market is the ultimate judge. Too many startups spend months or even years building a product based on internal assumptions, only to find out that customers don’t want it. The most critical tip for a successful product is to involve your users from day one. Conductinterviews, runsurveys, and create a feedback loop with your beta testers. Pay close attention to how they use your product, what they find confusing, and what they would like to see changed. Use this feedback to guide your development roadmap. Your product is not for you — it’s for them. By building a culture of listening and adapting, you ensure that your product evolves in a way that truly serves your customers’ needs. Don’t wait for perfection. Aminimum viable product (MVP)is the version of your product with the minimum set of features needed to deliver core value to early adopters. The goal of an MVP is to get a functional version into the hands of users as quickly as possible to validate your core hypothesis. This “release early, release often” approach is a fundamental principle of agile development. It allows you to: Test your assumptionsin the real world with real users.Gather valuable dataand feedback that will inform your next steps.Save money and timeby avoiding building features that no one will use.Start generating revenueand building a customer base sooner. The product development journey is not a sprint to the finish line, but a series of small, iterative loops. Launch your MVP, gather feedback, and continuously improve the product based on what you learn. This continuous cycle of improvement is the key to building a product that not only survives but thrives in the market.

raw_html:
You have a brilliant idea. A solution that will change how people work, play, or live. But an idea, no matter how great, is just the beginning. The journey from concept to a successful, market-ready product is paved with critical decisions and a lot of hard work. For startups, where every resource counts, a misstep in product development can be fatal.

The key to success isn’t just about building a product; it’s about building the right product. A product that solves a real problem for real people, in a way that’s better than the competition.

At Salesforce, we’ve seen countless startups grow from a handful of people with an idea into thriving, customer-centric businesses. We’ve learned that the most successful ones follow a disciplined, customer-focused approach. So here are five steps every startup should follow for successful product development.

What you’ll learn:
  • What is product development and why does your startup need it? 
  • Five product development steps to follow for success
  • Common startup product development strategies
  • And, 3 tips for successful product development
  • Recommended tools for product development
  • Ready to build, scale, and grow?

What is product development and why does your startup need it? 

Product development is the entire process of bringing a new product or service to market. For startups, it’s crucial because it allows them to:

  • Solve a problem: Startups are often born out of identifying an unmet need or a problem that existing solutions don’t adequately address. Product development is how they build something to solve that problem.
  • Gain a competitive edge: A well-developed, innovative product can differentiate a startup from larger, more established companies.
  • Attract investment: Investors look for startups with a clear vision and a viable product. A solid product development plan demonstrates that a startup knows how to execute its ideas.
  • Achieve market fit: Through product development, startups can iterate and refine their offerings based on user feedback, ensuring the product truly resonates with its target audience.
  • Generate revenue: Ultimately, successful product development leads to a product that customers want to buy, which is essential for a startup’s survival and growth.

Start with SMB Basics

Get the Free SMB Trends Report

How to Automate Your Small Business (AI Tips and Tools)

Win More Deals with Salesforce Starter Suite

Starter Suite vs. Pro Suite: CRM Comparison For SMBs

Five product development steps to follow for success

1. Start with the basics: Conduct market research with AI

Instead of relying on guesswork or limited data, startups can use artificial intelligence (AI) to conduct robust market research. AI tools can analyze vast datasets from social media, customer reviews, forums, and survey results to identify trends, pinpoint customer pain points, and segment target audiences with high accuracy. This allows you to validate your product idea and ensure you’re solving a real-world problem for a clearly defined group of people, saving you from building a product no one wants.

2. Try generative AI to supercharge ideation and design

Once you’ve validated your idea, AI can act as a co-pilot for the creative process. Generative AI tools can create multiple design variations and prototypes in minutes based on simple text prompts. You can quickly explore different user interfaces (UI), user experiences (UX), and even product concepts without a team of designers or costly software. This rapid prototyping reduces the time and cost of the early development phase, allowing you to iterate quickly and get to a functional minimum viable product (MVP) faster.

3. Simplify development and code generation with smart tools

AI isn’t just for planning and design; it can also accelerate the build phase. Vibe coding tools can generate code snippets, suggest optimizations, and even create entire applications based on your design specifications. This can significantly reduce development time and effort, especially for small teams with limited coding expertise. While a human is still needed to oversee and refine the code, AI handles the repetitive, time-consuming parts, allowing your developers to focus on complex, high-value tasks.

4. Breeze through quality assurance and testing

Manual testing is a bottleneck for many small businesses. AI-powered quality assurance (QA) tools can automate repetitive testing tasks, such as regression testing and visual UI comparisons. AI can also predict where bugs are most likely to occur by analyzing code changes and historical data, allowing your team to prioritize testing in high-risk areas. This leads to a more reliable product, fewer post-launch issues, and a faster time to market.

5. Use customer feedback for continuous improvement

The product development journey doesn’t end at launch. After your product is in the hands of customers, AI can provide a continuous feedback loop. AI tools can analyze customer reviews, support tickets, and social media mentions in real-time. Using natural language processing (NLP), AI can identify key themes, track sentiment, and categorize feedback automatically. This gives you a clear, data-driven understanding of what customers love and what needs improvement, allowing you to make informed decisions for future product updates and build a product that your customers will continue to love.

Build a loyal customer base with smarter, faster service solutions.

Common startup product development strategies

Successful startups don’t just build a product and hope for the best. They follow a strategic approach to product development that maximizes their limited resources and focuses on what truly matters: the customer. Here are three of the most common and effective product development strategies for startups.

The Lean Startup Methodology

The Lean Startup, popularized by entrepreneur Eric Ries, is a methodology that prioritizes speed and learning over extensive planning. It is built on a “Build-Measure-Learn” feedback loop that helps startups quickly validate their ideas and pivot when necessary.

  • Build: Instead of building a final, feature-complete product, the startup creates an MVP. This is the simplest version of the product with just enough features to be usable by early customers.
  • Measure: The startup then releases the MVP to a small group of users and collects data and feedback. This is a crucial step to “validate learning”—determining if the product is solving a real problem for the target audience.
  • Learn: Based on the data and feedback, the startup learns what’s working and what isn’t. This learning informs the next steps, which could be to “persevere” (continue building on the current path) or “pivot” (change direction based on the new insights).

The goal of the Lean Startup is to “fail fast,” meaning that if an idea is not viable, you discover it quickly and cheaply, rather than after months or years of wasted effort.

Agile Product Development

Agile is a flexible, iterative approach to product development that is well-suited for the dynamic and uncertain environment of a startup. Instead of a rigid, linear process (like the “waterfall” method), Agile breaks down the development work into small, manageable chunks called “sprints” or “iterations.”

  • Iterate: The team works in short cycles (typically one to four weeks) to deliver a working, usable increment of the product. This continuous delivery of small features allows for rapid feedback and adaptation.
  • Collaborate: A key principle of Agile is constant communication and collaboration with customers. This ensures that the product is always aligned with their evolving needs.
  • Adapt: The Agile Manifesto states, “Responding to change over following a plan.” This mindset allows startups to quickly incorporate new information, market shifts, and user feedback into their product roadmap without derailing the entire project.

Agile allows startups to release a product to market faster and reduce the risk of building something that doesn’t meet user requirements.

Design Thinking

Design Thinking is a human-centered problem-solving framework that puts the end-user at the heart of the product development process. It’s less about a rigid process and more about a mindset that encourages creative and innovative solutions. The process typically involves five stages:

  • Empathize: The startup immerses itself in the user’s world to understand their needs, challenges, and motivations. This can involve interviews, observations, and creating user personas.
  • Define: Based on the empathy phase, the startup synthesizes the information to create a clear, actionable problem statement. This ensures the team is focused on solving the right problem.
  • Ideate: This is a brainstorming phase where the team generates a wide range of creative ideas to solve the defined problem. No idea is too crazy, and the goal is to produce a high volume of potential solutions.
  • Prototype: The most promising ideas are turned into low-fidelity prototypes (like a paper sketch, a simple wireframe, or a basic model) that can be quickly tested.
  • Test: The prototypes are tested with real users to gather feedback and refine the solution. This is an iterative cycle, and it’s common to go back to an earlier stage (ideation or prototyping) based on the test results.

And, 3 tips for successful product development

1. Focus on solving a single, critical problem

For startups, the temptation to build a “Swiss army knife” of features is strong, but it’s a common pitfall. The most successful products start by doing one thing exceptionally well. Instead of trying to solve a dozen problems with an average solution, identify the single, most painful problem your target audience faces and create a product that provides the best possible solution to that specific issue. 

This singular focus allows you to channel your limited resources — time, money, and talent — into creating a product that is truly indispensable. Once you’ve dominated that niche and established a loyal user base, you can strategically add more features to expand your product’s capabilities.

2. Prioritize user feedback over assumptions

Your ideas are great, but the market is the ultimate judge. Too many startups spend months or even years building a product based on internal assumptions, only to find out that customers don’t want it. 

The most critical tip for a successful product is to involve your users from day one. Conduct interviews, run surveys, and create a feedback loop with your beta testers. Pay close attention to how they use your product, what they find confusing, and what they would like to see changed. Use this feedback to guide your development roadmap. Your product is not for you — it’s for them. By building a culture of listening and adapting, you ensure that your product evolves in a way that truly serves your customers’ needs.

3. Build a minimum viable product (MVP)

Don’t wait for perfection. A minimum viable product (MVP) is the version of your product with the minimum set of features needed to deliver core value to early adopters. The goal of an MVP is to get a functional version into the hands of users as quickly as possible to validate your core hypothesis. This “release early, release often” approach is a fundamental principle of agile development. It allows you to:

  • Test your assumptions in the real world with real users.
  • Gather valuable data and feedback that will inform your next steps.
  • Save money and time by avoiding building features that no one will use.
  • Start generating revenue and building a customer base sooner.

The product development journey is not a sprint to the finish line, but a series of small, iterative loops. Launch your MVP, gather feedback, and continuously improve the product based on what you learn. This continuous cycle of improvement is the key to building a product that not only survives but thrives in the market.

Recommended tools for product development

These tools are available to help you develop any type of product, from organization to execution. 

  • Salesforce: CRM for sales, service, marketing, commerce, and team collaboration
  • Jira: Project tracking and agile development
  • Figma: UI/UX design and prototyping
  • GitHub: Version control for code
  • Slack: Team communication and collaboration
  • Tableau: Data visualization and analytics
  • Optimizely: A/B testing and experimentation
  • FullStory: Session replay and user behavior analytics
  • Notion: Documentation and knowledge management
  • Zoom: Video conferencing
